channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Louisiana Killer’s Threats to Himself and Others Hinted at Future Violence
item.description: Shamar Elkins had been suffering severe mental health problems, his family said. His deadly rampage, which killed eight children, has profoundly scarred a community facing rising domestic violence.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina, Christina Morales and Jesus Jiménez
item.media:description: People attend a candlelight vigil in Shreveport, La., on Sunday.

item.title: Trump and Iran Face Off in Iran War Negotiations
item.description: As the United States and Iran make a second attempt at a deal, their negotiating styles are on a collision course.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: 2 Teens Killed and 5 Injured in North Carolina Shooting
item.description: The young people, planning for a fight, had arranged to meet off school grounds at a park in Winston-Salem, N.C., when gunfire broke out on Monday morning.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:description: The scene of a shooting on Monday at Leinbach Park in Winston-Salem, N.C.

item.title: Mother and 6 Children Die in Explosion at Their Pennsylvania Home
item.description: The blast on Sunday morning led to a fire that consumed the residence in a rural community northwest of Harrisburg, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Bernard Mokam
item.media:description: Firefighters at the scene of an explosion and fire on Sunday in Lamar Township, Pa.

item.title: Four Cabinet and other senior officials have now stepped down from Trump’s Cabinet.
item.dc:creator: Lily Boyce and Ashley Cai

item.title: A Rocky, Snow-Barren Ski Season Concludes in Colorado
item.description: Faced with a devastating snow drought and record-high March temperatures, some resorts closed early. But at Aspen Mountain, die-hards celebrated the last day of the season on Sunday.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri and Will Sardinsky

item.title: Scientist Irritated by Lab Colleague Accused in Poisoning Attempt
item.description: The scientist, at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, had built up grievances against his co-worker after five years of working together, court documents said.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: Lucy Hewett for The New York Times
item.media:description: The University of Wisconsin campus in Madison.

item.title: George Ariyoshi, 100, America’s First Governor of Asian Descent, Dies
item.description: A Democrat, he served three terms as Hawaii’s governor (1974-86), rising out of a tough district of Honolulu. He tried to diversify an economy heavily dependent on tourism.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden
item.media:description: George R. Ariyoshi speaking in Honolulu in 1977, during his first term as Hawaii’s governor. His election upended a tradition of white and Republican control in the state.

item.title: Lori Chavez-DeRemer, Trump’s Labor Secretary, Steps Down Amid Internal Investigation
item.description: Lori Chavez-DeRemer was facing multiple scandals and an internal inquiry into charges of misconduct.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer has been accused of having an affair with a member of her security team and using department resources for personal trips.

item.title: Democrats Once Loathed Gerrymandering. Now They’re Pushing for It.
item.description: The party is seeking an extra edge in Virginia at a time when its thinking has changed on the partisan drawing of political maps — in large part, leaders say, because of President Trump’s tactics.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti

item.title: Barbara Hatch Rosenberg, Who Joined Hunt for Anthrax Culprit, Dies at 97
item.description: An expert on biological weapons, she emerged as an influential voice during the F.B.I.’s rocky search for a suspect after the deadly anthrax attacks of 2001.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.category: State University of New York at Purchase

item.title: The Touska, the Iranian-Flagged Ship Seized by U.S. Forces, Was Under Sanctions
item.description: Marines are searching thousands of containers aboard the Touska, an Iranian cargo ship that the Navy disabled and seized on Sunday.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Strait of Hormuz off the coast of Musandam, Oman, last week.

item.title: House Ethics Panel Defends Handling of Sexual Misconduct Allegations
item.description: In urging victims to step forward, the secretive panel in charge of investigating charges of misconduct by members of Congress acknowledged limits that could allow offenses to go unaddressed.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:description: The Capitol last month.

item.title: Kansas Mayor Who Voted Illegally Will Not Serve Time in Prison
item.description: Joe Ceballos, a green card holder who resigned as Coldwater’s mayor after being charged, said he did not know that he had to be a citizen to vote.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Clayton Steward for The New York Times
item.media:description: Joe Ceballos, the former mayor of Coldwater, Kan.

item.title: Democrat Betty Yee Leaves the California Governor’s Race
item.description: Xavier Becerra, who once struggled to gain traction, has found growing support after Eric Swalwell left the race amid sexual harassment allegations.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Xavier Becerra, the former California attorney general, has had a resurgence after the sudden departure of Eric Swalwell from the governor’s race.

item.title: Kevin Warsh, Trump’s Fed Pick, to Defend Bank’s Ability to Set Rates Independently
item.description: Kevin M. Warsh is set to testify at the Senate Banking Committee on Tuesday even as a criminal investigation into the central bank risks delaying his ascension to become the next chair.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh is President Trump’s pick to lead the Federal Reserve.

item.title: A Brisk Day in Boston, for the Weather and Runners Alike
item.description: Boston held its 130th Marathon on a chilly day that was the crowd endured and the athletes relished.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:description: The finish of the Boston Marathon, at last.

item.title: Democratic Senators Fault Hegseth’s Leadership on Iran
item.description: In a letter, the 11 senators questioned the defense secretary’s decision to gut programs intended to protect civilians and said his orders endangered U.S. troops.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: The senators’ letter said several of Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s actions violated federal and international laws.

item.title: Louisiana Shooting: What We Know About the Killing of 8 Children in Shreveport
item.description: The shooting rampage on Sunday also left two adults wounded.
item.dc:creator: Billy Witz, Eduardo Medina and Christina Morales
item.media:description: A makeshift memorial in front of one of the homes where children were killed in Shreveport, La.

item.title: California Woman Is Arrested at LAX and Accused of Selling Weapons for Iran
item.description: Prosecutors said that Shamim Mafi, a legal permanent resident of the United States, helped facilitate millions of dollars in arms transfers from Iran.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The arrest of Shamim Mafi took place at Los Angeles International Airport on Saturday.

item.title: Kash Patel Sues The Atlantic for $250 Million Over Article Claiming Excessive Drinking
item.description: Mr. Patel accused the publication of defamation, asking for $250 million in damages. A spokeswoman for The Atlantic called the suit “meritless.”
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:description: Kash Patel, the F.B.I. director, sued The Atlantic.

item.title: Justices to Hear Case on Catholic Preschools That Reject Children of Gay Parents
item.description: Catholic preschools in Colorado that decline to enroll families with L.G.B.T.Q. children or parents sued to participate in a state-funded program.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.category: Same-Sex Marriage, Civil Unions and Domestic Partnerships
item.media:description: The Archdiocese of Denver campus in 2023. The archdiocese asked the Supreme Court to reconsider a First Amendment precedent unpopular with conservative Christians.

item.title: Vance Heads to New Talks With Iran. At Stake: Peace and His Own Standing.
item.description: The vice president is again center stage, after abruptly leaving the first round of high-level Iranian peace talks without an agreement.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance last week in Georgia. He is returning to Pakistan this week, after spending 21 hours last weekend negotiating with the Iranians.

item.title: Trump Administration Takes Steps to Refund $166 Billion in Tariffs
item.description: The government debuted a system to repay importers two months after the Supreme Court struck down tariffs at the heart of the president’s trade policy.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Ana Swanson
item.media:description: President Trump opposed tariff refunds until the Court of International Trade intervened in March and ordered the government to return the money to importers.

item.title: Big Names Wait in the Wings as Virginians Decide Their House Maps
item.description: With Virginians voting Tuesday to accept or reject redistricting, candidates from both parties await the voters’ judgment to decide whether — or where — to run for Congress.
item.dc:creator: Kate Zernike
item.media:credit: Danny Moloshok/Invision for the Television Academy, via Associated Press Images
item.media:description: Beth Macy, the best-selling author of books like “Dopesick,” is running for Congress in Virginia.

item.title: The Night the Government Closed the Skies Over El Paso
item.description: A high-energy laser weapon and a power struggle between federal agencies brought a night of hassles to the city.
item.dc:creator: Kate Kelly, Tawnell D. Hobbs, Reyes Mata III and Paul Ratje
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: The airspace around El Paso International Airport, including a roughly 11-mile diameter area from Sunland Park, N.M., to Horizon City, Texas, was temporarily closed in February for what the Federal Aviation Administration called “special security reasons.”

item.title: Epstein Wanted Connections at Harvard. Files Show Many Faculty Members Were Happy to Help.
item.description: New documents reveal what professors did to help Jeffrey Epstein get inside Harvard’s gates.
item.dc:creator: Mark Arsenault and Debra Kamin
item.media:credit: Lucy Lu for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jeffrey Epstein had a special obsession with Harvard, which he sought to infiltrate more than any other American institution.
